title: Apply: half-life and radiometric dating
description: Apply your understanding of half-life and radiometric dating in this set of free practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
Many volcanic rocks contain potassium, which allows scientists to date them using the radioactive decay of $\ce{K-40}$. This is how scientists discovered that the Galápagos Islands were only $3$ million years old. 

[[☃ image 1]]

Potassium-$40$ has a half-life of $1.25$ billion years. 

**If you start with $8.0$ grams of $\ce{K-40}$, how many grams will remain after $2.5$ billion years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay.

To answer the question, let's first determine how many half-lives have passed after $2.5$ billion years. To do this, we divide the number of years by the half-life:

$2.5$ billion years /$1.25$ billion years per half-life = $2$ half-lives.

So, two half-lives have passed.
How much $\ce{K-40}$ will be left after two half-lives? We started with $8.0$ grams, so after one half-life we'll have $4.0$ grams. After a second half-life we’ll have $2.0$ grams.
So, $2.0$ grams of $\ce{K-40}$ will remain.

options.explanation: “[La Cumbre volcano on Isla Fernandina](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:La_Cumbre_-_ISS.JPG)” by NASA, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)

options.alt: La Cumbre volcano on Isla Fernandina in the Galápagos Islands.
options.caption: *La Cumbre volcano on Isla Fernandina*

assessment_items.question_content:
Scientists use the radioisotope lead-$210$ to track the accumulation of snow on glacial ice. 

[[☃ image 1]]

Lead-$210$ has a half-life of $22.3$ years. 

**If you start with $24.0$ grams of $\ce{Pb-210}$, how many grams will remain after $67$ years?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay.

To answer the question, let's first determine how many half-lives have passed after $67$ years. To do this, we divide the number of years by the half-life:

$67$ years /$22.3$ years per half-life = $3$ half-lives.

So, three half-lives have passed.

How much $\ce{Pb-210}$ will be left after three half lives? We started with $24.0$ grams, so after one half-life we'll have $12.0$ grams. After a second half-life we’ll have $6.0$ grams. And, after a third half-life we'll have $3.0$ grams.
So, $3.0$ grams of $\ce{Pb-210}$ will remain.

options.explanation: “[Glacier on Antarctic coast](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Glacier_on_Antarctic_coast,_mountain_behind.jpg)” by Jason Auch, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)

options.alt: A glacier off the coast of Antarctica
options.caption: *Antarctic glacier*

assessment_items.question_content:
The *moai* are the famous stone heads of Rapa Nui, or Easter Island. A recent analysis of radiometric carbon dating has suggested that the heads were constructed over hundreds of years from 1100 to 1750 CE.

[[☃ image 1]]

Carbon-$14$ has a half-life of $5{,}730$ years. 

**If you start with $12.0$ grams $\ce{C-14}$, how many grams will remain after $17{,}190$ years?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay.

To answer the question, let's first determine how many half-lives have passed after $17{,}190$ years. To do this, we divide the number of years by the half-life:

$17{,}190$ years / $5{,}730$ years per half-life= $3$ half-lives.

So, three half-lives have passed.

How much $\ce{C-14}$ will be left after three half lives? We started with $12.0$ grams, so after one half-life we'll have $6.0$ grams. After a second half-life we’ll have $3.0$ grams. And, after a third half-life we'll have $1.5$ grams.
So, $1.5$ grams of $\ce{C-14}$ will remain.

options.explanation: “[Moai at Rano Raraku, Easter Island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Moai_Rano_raraku.jpg)” by Aurbina, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)

options.alt: Moai at Rano Raraku, Rapa Nui (Easter Island).
options.caption: *Moai at Rano Raraku*

assessment_items.question_content:
A scientist begins with $100.0$ grams of a radioactive isotope. After exactly $12$ days have passed, only $25.0$ grams remain.

**What is the half-life of this isotope?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] days
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay.

To answer the question, let’s first take our initial mass and see how many half lives it takes to reach a value around $25.0$ grams. We started with $100.0$ grams, so after one half-life we'll have $50.0$ grams. After a second half-life we’ll have $25.0$ grams. So two half-lives have passed.

Now let's figure out how long the half-life is. The total time it took for two half-lives to pass was $12$ days. Dividing $12$ days by $2$ gives us $6$ days. So, every $6$ days, the sample decreased by half.
So, the half-life of this isotope is $6$ days.

assessment_items.question_content:
A researcher begins with $60{,}000$ nuclei of a radioactive isotope. After exactly $ 6$ years have passed, only $15{,}000$ nuclei remain.

**What is the half-life of this isotope?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] years
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay.

To answer the question, let’s first take our initial amount and see how many half lives it takes to reach a value around $15{,}000$ nuclei. We started with $60{,}000$ nuclei, so after one half-life we'll have $30{,}000$ nuclei. After a second half-life we’ll have $15{,}000$ nuclei. So two half-lives have passed.

Now let's figure out how long the half-life is. The total time it took for two half-lives to pass was $6$ years. Dividing $6$ years by $2$ gives us $3$ years. So, every $3$ years, the sample decreased by half.
So, the half-life of this isotope is $3$ years.

assessment_items.question_content:
A nuclear physicist begins with $2.00$ moles of a radioactive isotope. After exactly $36$ days have passed, only $0.25$ moles remain.

**What is the half-life of this isotope?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] days
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay.

To answer the question, let’s first take our initial amount and see how many half lives it takes to reach a value around $0.25$ moles. We started with $2.00$ moles, so after one half-life we'll have $1.00$ moles. After a second half-life we’ll have $0.50$ moles. After a third half-life, we’d have $0.25$ moles. So three half-lives have passed.

Now let's figure out how long the half-life is. The total time it took for three half-lives to pass was $36$ days. Dividing $36$ days by $3$ gives us $12$ days. So, every $12$ days, the sample decreased by half.
So, the half-life of this isotope is $12$ days.

assessment_items.question_content:
A radioactive isotope was prepared in a laboratory. The following graph tracks its decay over a period of $24$ days. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the approximate half-life of this radioisotope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay. Let's use the graph to identify this value for the isotope described above.
The graph shows how the number of nuclei in the sample changes over time. It shows that at $0$ days (when the sample was prepared), there were $16{,}000$ nuclei in the sample.

Half of this amount is $8{,}000$ nuclei. Starting at $8{,}000$ nuclei on the *y*-axis, we can read across the graph to intersect the decay curve. Then, we can read down to find the time (in days) on the *x*-axis. The sample decays to $8{,}000$ nuclei at Day $8$. 

[[☃ image 1]]
So, the half-life of the isotope is $8$ days.

options.alt: A radioactive decay graph showing the decay of 16,000 nuclei over 24 days.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following graph shows the decay of a pure sample of radioisotope over a period of $10$ years. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the approximate half-life of this radioisotope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay. Let's use the graph to identify this value for the isotope described above.
The graph shows how the mass of the sample changes over time. It shows that at $0$ days (when the measuring began), the sample had a mass of $24.0$ grams.

Half of this amount is $12.0$ grams. Starting at $12.0$ grams on the *y*-axis, we can read across the graph to intersect the decay curve. Then, we can read down to find the time (in years) on the *x*-axis. The sample decays to $12.0$ grams at $2.0$ years.

[[☃ image 1]]
So, the half-life of the isotope is $2.0$ years.

options.alt: A radioactive decay graph showing the decay of 24 grams of radioisotope over 10 years.

assessment_items.question_content:
A nuclear lab has produced $0.50$ moles of a pure radioisotope. The graph below shows it decaying over the course of $36$ hours. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the approximate half-life of this radioisotope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay. Let's use the graph to identify this value for the isotope described above.
The graph shows how the mass of the sample changes over time. It shows that at $0$ hours (when the sample was prepared), there was $0.50$ moles of sample.

Half of this amount is $0.25$ moles. Starting at $0.25$ moles on the *y*-axis, we can read across the graph to intersect the decay curve. Then, we can read down to find the time (in hours) on the *x*-axis. The sample decays to $0.25$ moles at $6$ hours.

[[☃ image 1]]
So, the half-life of the isotope is $6$ hours.

options.alt: A radioactive decay graph showing the decay of 0.50 moles of nuclei over 36 hours

assessment_items.question_content:
The following graph shows three different radioisotopes decaying over a period of $10$ seconds. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which isotope has the shortest half-life?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay. The *faster* an isotope decays, the *shorter* its half-life.

The graph shows the decay of three different isotopes: A, B, and C. By looking at the *y*-axis (which shows the fraction of parent isotope remaining), we can see that all three curves start at the same point, $100$%. However, the curves show us that each isotope decays at a different rate.

To determine which isotope decays the fastest, we can find the one that reaches half of the starting amount ($50$%) first. Starting at $50$% on the *y*-axis, we can read across the graph to intersect the decay curves. Then, we can read down to find the corresponding times on the *x*-axis. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The curve that reaches $50$% in the *shortest* amount of time has the shortest half-life.
Isotope C has the shortest half-life.

options.alt: A graph showing three different decay curves.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following graph shows three different radioisotopes decaying over a period of $10$ seconds.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which isotope has the longest half-life?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay. The *slower* an isotope decays, the *longer* its half-life.

The graph shows the decay of three different isotopes: A, B, and C. By looking at the *y*-axis (which shows the fraction of parent isotope remaining), we can see that all three curves start at the same point, $100$%. However, the curves show us that each isotope decays at a different rate.

To determine which isotope decays the slowest, we can find the one that reaches half of the starting amount ($50$%) last. Starting at $50$% on the *y*-axis, we can read across the graph to intersect the decay curves. Then, we can read down to find the corresponding times on the *x*-axis. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The curve that reaches $50$% in the *longest* amount of time has the longest half-life.
Isotope A has the longest half-life.

options.alt: A graph showing three different decay curves.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following graph shows three different radioisotopes decaying over a period of $10$ seconds.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Move the tiles to rank the isotopes from shortest to longest half-life. **

Shortest half-life

[[☃ sorter 1]]

Longest half-life
hints: Half-life is the time it takes for half of a radioisotope sample to decay. The *faster* an isotope decays, the *shorter* its half-life.

The graph shows the decay of three different isotopes: A, B, and C. By looking at the *y*-axis (which shows the fraction of parent isotope remaining), we can see that all three curves start at the same point, $100$%. However, the curves show us that each isotope decays at a different rate.

To order the isotopes by decreasing half-life, we can compare when each isotope reaches half of its starting amount ($50$%). Starting at $50$% on the *y*-axis, we can read across the graph to intersect the decay curves. Then, we can read down to find the corresponding times on the *x*-axis.

[[☃ image 1]]

The curve that reaches $50$% in the *shortest* amount of time has the *shortest* half-life. Similarly, the curve that reaches $50$% in the *longest* amount of time has the *longest* half-life.
So, ranking the isotopes in increasing order from shortest to longest half-life gives us C, B, A.

options.alt: A graph showing three different decay curves.
